sentences.text: Pardon?
Yeah, I'm all right.
Thank you, Susie.
Mhm.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, well, had been killed.
I mean, when we landed, mostly first the group, the initial landing parties were well-- not well inland.
But they were already a few kilometers inland.
And I say, I think the Canadians landed the same place as us.
I think it was the second Canadian Army landed at the same time as the British troops.
So you just saw them, the Canadian and British troops, laying in the sand, dead, or being washed up by the sea and against the beach.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, a place called Ver-sur-Mer.
It was a small village.
Well, first you had go on the beach.
You had to land on the beach.
And you had to take off all your waterproofing equipment, which was on the bulldozer.
Most of the tanks were all waterproofed.
So you had to prepare your equipment to go forward.
No.
Only when we were coming off the LST.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, things are a little vague, you know?
A little-- but I remember that once we did land and took the waterproofing equipment off the bulldozers and the equipment, I think we rested a while.
I don't know how long for.
It couldn't have been very, very long.
Then we moved forward, going towards Bayeux.
Yeah, from the-- from Arromanches, from beach Arromanches, we went into this place called Ver-sur-Mer.
No.
[INAUDIBLE]
I don't know.
Might've escaped, run away.
I don't know.
Do you know, first time I ever saw a civilian, when we went into Bayeux.
But this little place, Ver-sur-Mer, was picked out as one of the places where we had to build an airfield.
And I think the second airfield--
The second airfield in Normandy was built at this place called Ver-sur-Mer.
Oh yeah.
Don't worry.
One or two days, that's all.
Two or three days.
I can't remember.
No, no, we were dug in, slit trenches.
You know?
The first days, no, fair.
I remember it was summer there.
Only field rations.
Pardon?
Is that what you ate?
No, we went through to Bayeux.
We didn't stay there.
We went into Bayeux.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we moved to a place called [INAUDIBLE] Shores.
Ah, yes, still Normandy.
When it is?
So they'd only just been captured.
And then we-- we had to dig in a place called Jerusalem [INAUDIBLE].
And I actually got wounded there, in my leg, by mortar fire.
Yeah.
We were being bombarded by the Germans.
Because they were fighting very, very strongly there at the time.
No, we'd seen some civilians, like in Bayeux.
In this place, Jerusalem, where we were dug in, it was a farming area.
And we did see some farmers there.
You know?
Some French people there.
And-- [SIGHS]
Pardon?
They could have done.
I don't know.
I never took a German prisoner myself.
But I saw plenty of them being-- being taken back to the beaches.
Fair.
Yeah, never-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think we ever abused German prisoners.
Well, they were all battles.
I mean--
Well, the Battle of Caen.
We were dug in a place called Carpiquet, which was an airfield, which we tried to capture.
Because we-- we needed a airfield.
But unfortunately, the Germans were dug in very, very strong there.
They had one of their SS regiments there, and they're defending this town, which was a main town to sort of break out of Normandy.
So I was in this Battle of Carpiquet.
Well, a big deal, scared, frightened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just kept my fingers crossed, I suppose.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's right.
But I mean, being a soldier doesn't necessarily mean you're always in the infantry.
I wasn't in infantry.
I was in the field regiment.
And we were always close to the infantry.
Normally they would be ahead of us.
But obviously, you got involved in fighting.
If you were-- it was very close.
Yeah.
I had two of my friends get killed.
In-- one in Jerusalem with me.
And one in Carpiquet.
There was a lot that was killed.
But there were these two particular friends I had.
Funnily enough, one of them was called Max [?
Bygraves. ?] Not the-- the actor.
One of them called--
Yeah, they were buried in Jerusalem, at this place in Jerusalem.
It was a very, very small place.
I think you'd have a difficultly finding it on the map.
Very, very small village, which was either north or south of Tilly-sur-Seulles.
I can't remember now.
And I lost a friend at Carpiquet as well.
Oh yeah.
Oh yeah, not actual fighting.
But I certainly came in contact with them.
I saw plenty of them dead.
Yeah, the ones that were captured.
I never got involved in hand-to-hand fighting.
That was a World War I system of fighting.
It was never that kind of fighting.
Maybe except for on special certain occasions like Battle of Arnhem, where they engaged in hand-to-hand fighting.
But there was very little hand-to-hand fighting in the part of Normandy I was.
Yeah.
Oh, I dare not talk about the French people.
I didn't particularly like 'em.
They-- I mean, a lot of them had got married to German soldiers.
They had built families together there during the whole occupy-- occupied by German-- by Germany.
No.
No.
The only time we orders we got was when we went into Germany.
And there was no fraternization.
That was after the war finished, where we couldn't fraternize.
No, no, no.
Per se, we didn't be in contact with them all that much.
I mean, most places we went through had been captured, had been destroyed, the villages.
No, I think there was too much turmoil.
And I mean, yeah, there's some things that happened and you can't recollect how they happened and why it happened.
Is there anything you could think of?
Do you--
Strange enough, no.
I can't recollect seeing any children.
I think-- yeah, women, yeah.
But I think most of the population, they'd evacuated.
I mean, there was a big battle going on around them.
I don't think they'd stay.
Yeah, I went into Belgium.
Leon, ?] Ghent.
It's called Gand, G-A-N-D.
But it's spelled G-H-E-N-T, Ghent.
A little place south of Brussels that we were dug in.
But I can't remember the name.
It was an airfield quite close to there.
I think it was the first time I had some RR, rest and recreation, was in Ghent.
Went into the town, drank, then went back to camp.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, I met a Belgian doctor.
He was a Jewish doctor.
You remember a few things, you see them, and you go on your way.
I mean, probably a lot of events happened that I can't even recollect today.
Only the most important ones, you know, you can recollect.
No, all had been captured.
Yeah.
Cleaning up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, cleaning up, mainly building roads, rebuilding roads, rebuilding airfields if they needed rebuilding.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mhm.
That-- that was the main function of the sort of regiment I was in at the time.
Oh, a couple of days, few days.
Then we moved on, I think, into Holland.
First place I remember in Holland was actually Tilburg, that we'd captured.
Captured the same day that we entered it.
They'd sort of run off, gone somewhere else.
Pardon?
No, I didn't see any.
I didn't see any.
There could have been.
But I didn't see.
You don't-- you don't always see these things in the army.
There's always special units that are given this kind of job, you know, moving prisoners to the-- to the rear of the lines.
Well, in Tilburg, we stayed there for two or three days.
But the second day or first day we were there, we were given a-- what they call a company order, where they wanted volunteers, or Jewish volunteers.
And there was only two Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] in my unit.
There was myself and a fellow called Dave Goldstein.
And apparently the small local shul in Tilburg had been desecrated by the Germans.
So they asked for volunteers to help clean up this little shul there, or synagogue.
But there was quite a few of us, even, like, non-Jewish soldiers who
--volunteered to do the job.
And it's whilst we were at this shul, in this place cleaning up, that I met two families.
One was called Esse.
And one was called [?
Pollack. ?] [?
Pollack ?] was the cousins of this family, Esse.
They were in with-- we met them whilst we were cleaning up this shul.
And of course, they were some of the Jewish families that were left behind there, who were also clean-- cleaning up the shul there, cleaning up the synagogue there.
And I got to know these people.
I spoke to them.
I took them food.
As a matter of fact, I've still got-- found some letters that they wrote to my wife, thanking me, you know, thanking us for supplying them with food.
Oh yeah, I still remember the address.
They-- they lived at a place called 23 [INAUDIBLE], in Tilburg.
I don't know what his occupation was.
I think he might have been a merchant or something.
And his wife, [?
Yeta, ?] they didn't have any children at that time.
And I don't know if they were hidden.
I-- I-- I was told that they managed to hide themselves in somewhere in Tilburg, with them and the other family, [?
Pollack. ?] And I think after three days we left.
And they communicated with me, following up.
They wrote letters to me whilst I was still in Holland, or whilst I was still in the forces.
And they did write a couple of letters to my wife here in London.
I think there was a few.
But they were the only two families I remember, or--
Or who I communicated with.
No.
I-- yet-- oh, incidentally, they-- William Esse, a man, actually gave me his yellow Star of David, which I donated to the Jewish museum here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I don't know what happened.
Yeah.
Yeah, were in hiding.
No, they just said, they'd managed to sort of keep away from the Germans, who were occupied in Tilburg at the time.
Oh yeah, of course they were.
Yeah, most of the Dutch people were.
Good.
Good.
We always treated them with courtesy and with care, and with a bit of pity as well, what had happened there.
You know?
I was treated well, by what?
The British?
Oh, yeah.
Oh yeah.
Oh yeah.
When we came as liberators, and I don't think they could've treated us otherwise.
No.
They didn't have any food.
We gave them food.
I gave them food.
From [LAUGHS] the canteen.
And I had a sergeant friend who-- from the kitchen.
You know?
I managed to get some food.
Of course, I had my own food as well, my rations, which I gave to them.
Yeah.
The chocolate, I think.
[LAUGHS] The chocolate.
The-- yeah, I think chocolate mainly.
And then they always asked for cigarettes, of course.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, after the-- [? when ?] I came back to England, yeah, communicated with them.
No, soldiers were-- can't explain.
Maybe civilians do, but soldiers very rarely hear these things.
Mainly because you're on the move the whole time.
And your main purpose, I suppose, is you tried to stay alive.
What happened?
Well, we entered Germany from a place called Xanten or Wesel.
And I think the first place-- I was trying to remember what it was.
Oh yeah, funnily enough, [INAUDIBLE] going into Germany, we-- on our way from Xanten, we came to a place called Kleve.
Kleve.
And from there I was attached to the American Ninth Army.
Not me personally, but the camp that I was in was attached to the American Ninth Army for two or three weeks.
And we were the American Ninth Army then.
And the main purpose of this was to learn their methods of making airfields, repairing airfields, and repairing roads.
So we were testing them as-- on a [? sort ?] [? of ?] training course.
They were more efficient.
And they'd done it quicker, and they had the equipment.
We didn't.
Mostly equipment, you know?
I mean, they had things for making roads which we didn't have, and the American army-- or the Americans supplied the British army with.
So their methods were a lot more efficient than ours and quicker.
Yeah.
I met a few Jewish boys in the American army.
Yeah.
And I won't say too much about the American Army.
Because maybe I had three weeks with them.
I said, I won't say too much about the American army.
But--
Yeah.
So I went to Kleve, [INAUDIBLE] to Xanten, [INAUDIBLE].
Then from there, let's see, I went to Munster.
A town called Munster.
I think we only passed through there.
And then there we went to another place called Rain.
And I did pick up some artifacts there.
I picked up-- it was lying in the road, or lying somewhere.
It was a Hitlerjugend [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] membership form.
And I picked up some medals there.
I picked up some medals from some dead German soldiers, which I gave to the Jewish Museum.
And then from there, there were-- this-- this is-- the only thing that's a little vague with me at the moment, it was either from Munster or Brunswick that we went to Bergen-Belsen.
Mhm.
Yeah, Munster was a large town.
What's the army function there?
You're just going through there.
My particular function was nothing.
We just passed through there, at Munster.
I think I stayed-- we stayed there for probably a day.
The soldiers who were on the move sometimes didn't always have something to do.
You know?
No, no, no.
No, no.
I was only with the American forces for about two or three weeks, with the American Ninth Army.
And after-- I left the American Ninth.
I went back to our-- not regiment-- to our division, that we went onto Munster.
No.
No idea.
No.
That's a very difficult thing--
No, I really don't remember.
Well, it must've been.
Of course, I think after that, we went on to Brunswick or Hannover.
Days, you know?
Not any length of time.
No, no.
No.
The officers had maps.
But not the ordinary sap or private soldier.
Oh yeah, of course we knew.
Yeah, we'd heard about them.
Of course we had.
Pardon?
Well, you've got to remember, we had army newspapers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had American newspapers, army newspapers.
And of course, we read it from there.
And of course the mere fact that I heard from my friends, the Esses, we'd learned-- we'd learned quite a bit.
You know, what happened to the Jewish population, and all that.
Oh yeah.
Yeah.
Well, after were-- we were already aware of the concentration camps.
We were aware of them and what the Germans were doing.
I couldn't believe it.
I think they felt the same as I did.
We felt, obviously, quite a lot of hatred to the Germans, especially what they'd done in France and Belgium.
You know?
So we'd heard all about that.
Yeah.
No.
I've still got letters form my wife and all that.
And funnily enough, it wasn't mentioned.
Why, I don't know.
Maybe because of the censors.
I don't know.
Because your mail was censored at a time.
Yeah.
But of course, I-- you couldn't always write.
You weren't allowed to say where you were.
All you could write, say, whether you were fit and well.
You know, and it was very difficult.
[INAUDIBLE] some-- some letters were terribly censored.
I remember I once wrote a letter to my wife.
After I came back from the army, I got demobilized from the army.
She showed me this letter.
About three quarters of it was cut out.
And I can't remember if I'd ever mentioned anything that you could call security reasons.
But it was censored, very heavily censored.
Which makes sense, in any case.
Yes.
I remember they said that we were going into one of the camps.
And I think at the time they said, we're going to a place called Bergen-Belsen, which we didn't attach the Belsen.
And then we heard [INAUDIBLE] again, was Bergen on the Hill, or Belsen, or Bergen on the Hill, or Belsen on the Hill.
Yeah.
We-- we had heard.
Well, that it was a concentration camp.
We did hear that.
And that-- well, all the things that you could hear about a concentration camp, I suppose we heard.
You know?
And you'd have to go ahead and do it, irrespective of the circumstances or what was going on.
But I suppose as a Jewish soldier, I had great feelings of qualm and uneasiness.
You know, I thought I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't actually explain-- describe my feelings, not at the time.
It's only when I actually got into the camp, that after, I felt-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] disbelief.
You know?
What I saw.
Had the military prepared you for what you might see?
Well, they told us it was a concentration camp.
They told us it was notorious.
Yeah.
They said probably what you'll see may shock you.
But we were there to clean up the place, clear up, and all that.
I think they were.
We were-- I-- I went in there with my field regiment, I think a day before it was actually-- day after the first British troops had got in there.
Yeah, April.
I think the end of April or May.
I can't remember.
I think it must have been any time between April and May, 1945.
By truck.
No, no, with my-- with my company, field regiment.
I remember going through a big gate.
And you could see a lot of smoke.
You could smell the place.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But we entered the place, it was just a shambles, people laying dead or half dead.
I remember seeing some great big pits that had been dug by the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think just disbelief, actually-- hard to describe, Susie.
Yeah, I think we were the second.
We might've been the first.
I don't even know, Susie.
I'll be quite honest.
We might have been the first British troops in there.
But when we were there, British troops were already in there.
So we might've just been on the tail end, just behind them.
I don't know.
Of course, movements were very, very hard to distinguish in those days, you know.
I can't remember.
I can't remember.
Too far back.
But I know we went through some two big gates though.
So I remember the place.
Where, German?
Yeah.
But I think they'd already been held captive.
So we might've just been on the second line that went in there, the second body of British soldiers that went in there.
I do-- do remember, when they brought out Josef Kramer and Irma Grese, and the other lady wardens.
And there was some men wardens at-- I just caught glimpses of them as they were brought out.
From there, I don't know where they went.
Not with respect.
Yeah, OK.
